holding: The Fourth Amendment protects people, not places: a person has a 'reasonable expectation of privacy' even in a public phone booth.
whyItMatters: Created the reasonable-expectation-of-privacy test that underlies which conversations count as 'private' (and thus protected) in most all-party recording statutes.

holding: No reasonable expectation of privacy in information voluntarily shared with a third party (the numbers you dial), so a pen register was not a 'search'.
whyItMatters: The 'third-party doctrine' — once you share information with someone, your privacy interest weakens. Relevant to how courts treat metadata and shared communications.

holding: The First Amendment protects publishing a recording of a matter of public concern even when someone else illegally intercepted it, where the publisher was not involved in the illegal recording.
whyItMatters: Distinguishes the act of recording (which can be illegal) from publishing its contents (which can be protected) — a key line for journalists and leakers, not a license to record.

holding: Citizens have a First Amendment right to openly record police officers performing their duties in public.
whyItMatters: Carves out a narrow public-interest exception: recording public officials in public is generally protected — but this does not extend to private meetings or calls.

holding: Accessing historical cell-site location information is a Fourth Amendment search requiring a warrant, limiting the third-party doctrine for sensitive digital data.
whyItMatters: Signals that courts are reining in the third-party doctrine as technology makes pervasive tracking and aggregation possible — the backdrop for modern always-on recording tools.

holding: California's all-party consent law can apply to protect a California resident even when the recording party is in a one-party-consent state (Georgia).
whyItMatters: The leading reason a cross-state recorder cannot rely on their own state's one-party rule: a stricter state's law may apply to protect its resident. Default to all-party on interstate calls.
